target.title: feat(autoreview): support cursor-agent engine
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The repo-local autoreview helper supported Codex, Claude, Droid, and Copilot review engines, but could not run Cursor's headless `cursor-agent` as a reviewer.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds `cursor-agent` as an explicit opt-in autoreview engine while keeping Codex as the helper default unless the environment selects another engine. The Cursor integration mirrors the existing hardened engine pattern: prompts are passed through stdin, the reviewed repository is not used as the trusted workspace, unsupported flags fail closed, streaming output is filtered for readable progress, and embedded JSON report parsing tolerates Cursor preface text without accepting malformed reports.

## User Impact

Users can run autoreview with `--engine cursor-agent` or `AUTOREVIEW_ENGINE=cursor-agent`. Existing Codex/Claude/Droid/Copilot behavior and defaults are unchanged.

## Evidence

- `python -m py_compile .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/test-review-harness.py`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/test-review-harness --fixture benign --engine cursor-agent`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/test-review-harness --fixture malicious --engine cursor-agent`
- `AUTOREVIEW_ENGINE=cursor-agent .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode commit --commit HEAD`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: Suppress expired exec approval followup warnings
target.body:
## Summary

Suppress exec approval followup warning logs when the approval id is already gone (`unknown or expired approval id`).

## Why

During investigation of #66675, restart/reporting flows could leave the gateway healthy while adding noisy log lines like:

- `exec approval followup dispatch failed`
- `unknown or expired approval id`

This patch does **not** claim to fully solve the false non-zero restart on its own, but it removes one clearly benign error path that can fire after approval lifecycle completion and muddy the operator signal.

OpenClaw already has `isApprovalNotFoundError(...)` for this exact class of benign approval lookup failure in other paths, so this makes followup dispatch handling consistent with the rest of the codebase.

## Change

- import and use `isApprovalNotFoundError(...)` in `sendExecApprovalFollowupResult(...)`
- silently ignore approval-not-found followup dispatch errors
- continue deduped warning logs for real failures like missing channel, transport errors, etc.


## Validation

```bash
pnpm check:test-types
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.test.ts
git diff --check
node --import tsx tmp/pr66685-proof.mjs
```

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Expired/already-resolved exec approval followup dispatches could log noisy warnings (`exec approval followup dispatch failed` / `unknown or expired approval id`) even though the approval lifecycle failure is benign. The fix suppresses only approval-not-found followup errors and keeps real transport failures warning.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout for PR #66685 rebased onto current `origin/main` on Linux/Node 22, using the patched production `sendExecApprovalFollowupResult(...)` helper from `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.ts`. The proof used redacted approval/session/account/chat identifiers.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node --import tsx tmp/pr66685-proof.mjs` from the patched checkout. The script invoked the production followup-result helper for an expired approval-not-found followup response, then invoked the same helper with a non-approval transport failure to confirm real warnings are still preserved.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the after-fix command:

  ```text
  $ node --import tsx tmp/pr66685-proof.mjs
  expired approval followup warning count: 0
  expired approval followup suppressed: true
  real followup failure warning preserved: true
  warning event redacted: exec approval followup dispatch failed (id=approval_redacted_expired): transport unavailable after redaction
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The expired approval followup produced zero warning logs, while the non-approval failure still emitted the expected `exec approval followup dispatch failed` warning, so the suppression is limited to the benign approval-not-found lifecycle case.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a full live UI/chat approval lifecycle with a real expired approval id; this proof directly exercises the production followup-result helper with redacted runtime-shaped inputs, and the focused unit/type/format checks remain supplemental.

## AI assistance

AI-assisted PR. I understand what the change does. I attempted the recommended local `codex review --base origin/main`, but the local Codex CLI returned `Quota exceeded`; GitHub CI/review checks still ran on the PR.

## Issue

- Related to #66675

target.title: fix(telegram): tell models to use <tg-math> for rich-text formulas
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a problem where Telegram bot replies that contain math formulas render as literal text instead of formatted math.

When Telegram rich text is enabled, the agent receives a capability hint listing the available Bot API 10.1 features. That hint advertises **"formulas"** but never says how to write one. As a result, models emit math using the LaTeX delimiters they were trained on — `$...$`, `$$...$$`, or `\(...\)` / `\[...\]` — none of which Telegram Bot API 10.1 rich messages treat as math. The formula shows up verbatim (e.g. `( a^2 + b^2 = c^2 )` or a raw `\int_...` source line) in the chat.

This is model-dependent and bites fallback models hardest: in my setup the primary model happened to emit `$...$` while the fallback model emitted `\(...\)`, so formulas silently broke only when the fallback was active.

## Why This Change Was Made

Telegram Bot API 10.1 renders formulas only through dedicated tags, with the body treated as **raw LaTeX**:

- inline: `<tg-math>x^2 + y^2</tg-math>`
- block: `<tg-math-block>E = mc^2</tg-math-block>`

(See the HTML formatting section of <https://core.telegram.org/bots/api> — "Formula source is treated as raw LaTeX".)

OpenClaw's own Telegram converter already allowlists exactly these tags (`extensions/telegram/src/format.ts` lists `tg-math` / `tg-math-block`, and `format.test.ts` preserves `<tg-math>E=mc^2</tg-math>` and `<tg-math-block>\int_0^1 x^2 dx</tg-math-block>`). The only gap was that the model-facing hint didn't mention them, so models had to guess — and guessed wrong.

This change spells out the tag syntax in the hint, matching the existing HTML-tag style already used there for `<sup>`/`<sub>`, `<mark>`, and `<details>`. It also explicitly steers models away from the two delimiter forms they most commonly default to.

**Scope / non-goals:** this is a prompt-accuracy fix only. A heavier alternative — converting `$...$` / `\(...\)` to `<tg-math>` in the markdown→rich-HTML pipeline so models can keep using natural LaTeX delimiters — was considered and intentionally not done here, because it adds a Telegram-specific math parser and risks corrupting `$`/`\(` inside code blocks, inline code, and currency text. Happy to follow up with that as a separate feature if maintainers prefer the converter-side approach.

## User Impact

Bots with Telegram rich text enabled now get correctly rendered math formulas regardless of which model (primary or fallback) produced the reply, because the model is told the exact `<tg-math>` / `<tg-math-block>` syntax instead of guessing.

## Evidence

- **Telegram spec:** official Bot API HTML formatting defines `<tg-math>` / `<tg-math-block>` as the formula tags (raw LaTeX body).
- **Existing OpenClaw contract:** `extensions/telegram/src/format.ts` allowlists `tg-math`/`tg-math-block`; `extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts` ("preserves rich table, details, quote, checklist, anchor, and math HTML") asserts both tags pass through unchanged.
- **Observed failure before the change:** a fallback model emitted `\( a^2 + b^2 = c^2 \)` and `\[ ... \]`; these rendered as literal `(...)` / raw LaTeX in Telegram rather than as math.
- **Change is confined to a single prompt string literal.** I evaluated the edited literal to confirm the model now sees `formulas (inline \`<tg-math>LaTeX</tg-math>\`, block \`<tg-math-block>LaTeX</tg-math-block>\`; not \`$...$\` or \`\(...\)\`)`. The prompt unit tests that assert on this hint (`src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`) check for the leading `"Telegram rich text is available"` sentence, which is unchanged.

Note on testing: I did not run the full monorepo suite locally (no `node_modules` in my checkout); the change is a single capability-hint string with no logic change.


## Real Behavior Proof (after-fix render)

Live Telegram render of a reply containing the hinted tags, captured on Telegram **iOS** (native rich-message rendering):

![tg-math rendered proof](https://blog.wangwllu.com/s/pr97197-tgmath-proof.jpg)

- inline `<tg-math>E=mc^2</tg-math>` → renders as typeset *E = mc²*
- block `<tg-math-block>\int_0^1 x^2\,dx = \tfrac{1}{3}</tg-math-block>` → renders as a centered display integral with limits and fraction

Both tags render as real typeset math (not literal `<tg-math>` source), confirming end-to-end that the syntax this PR tells models to emit is the syntax the Telegram rich-message path actually renders. This is the missing piece flagged in the prior review (patch quality was already cleared; only real-render proof was outstanding).

target.title: [codex] fix devices approve local fallback
target.body:
## Summary
- rebase the device approval fix onto current `main` after the recent gateway-side pairing changes
- keep `devices approve` on the Gateway-first path so Gateway authorization, approval logging, and `device.pair.resolved` broadcasts are preserved
- disable local pairing fallback when explicit `--url`, `--token`, or `--password` flags are provided, with CLI tests and docs coverage

## Root cause / resolution
The original local-first approval change is no longer the right shape on current `main`: it could bypass Gateway approval side effects and device-token authorization checks. This update keeps approval routed through `device.pair.approve` first and only allows the existing local loopback fallback for implicit local credentials. When a caller provides explicit Gateway/auth flags, the CLI now stays on the Gateway-authenticated path instead of silently reading local pairing files.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Explicit `--token` auth on a local loopback `devices list` command must stay on the Gateway-authenticated path and must not silently fall back to local pairing files.
- **Real environment tested:** Local macOS source checkout on this rebased branch, with a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and a current-branch loopback gateway started on `127.0.0.1:18891` using `pnpm openclaw gateway run --bind loopback --port 18891 --force --allow-unconfigured`.
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Started the temporary gateway, waited for the port to bind, then ran `pnpm openclaw devices list --json --token [REDACTED_TOKEN]` with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=18891` and the same temporary config/state environment.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output and gateway log excerpt from the live local run:

```text
$ pnpm openclaw devices list --json --token [REDACTED_TOKEN]
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: unauthorized: gateway token mismatch (set gateway.remote.token to match gateway.auth.token)
[openclaw] Could not start the CLI.
[openclaw] Reason: gateway closed (1008): unauthorized: gateway token mismatch (set gateway.remote.token to match gateway.auth.token)
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18891
Source: local loopback
Config: [TMPDIR]/openclaw.json
Bind: loopback

$ tail -n 30 gateway.log
[gateway] http server listening (9 plugins: acpx, bonjour, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 3.3s)
[gateway] ready
[ws] unauthorized ... client=cli cli v2026.5.8 role=operator scopes=1 auth=token device=yes ... reason=token_mismatch
[ws] closed before connect ... code=1008 reason=connect failed
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The explicit-token CLI call failed through Gateway auth with `token_mismatch` on the local loopback gateway. The output did not include `Direct scope access failed; using local fallback.`, and the gateway log recorded `auth=token`, which shows the command remained on the explicit Gateway auth path.
- **What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

## Tests
- `pnpm test src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/devices.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save
target.body:
# OpenClaw session-store path fix, 2026-04-12

## Status
Applied live to installed runtime. Also ported carefully into a fresh source clone of the matching `v2026.4.11` upstream tag, but not yet merged upstream or installed from that source tree.

## Source clone
- Repo: `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/external/openclaw-upstream`
- Branch: `fix/session-store-transcript-path-canonicalization`

## Protected file
`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/store-Cw5RrVCw.js`

## Backup
`/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/backups/runtime-patches/store-Cw5RrVCw.js.pre-20260412-1233`

## What changed
Added a save-time canonicalization step so persisted `sessionFile` and `transcriptPath` values are rewritten into the real sessions directory before session-store writes hit disk.

## Why
Without this, restored/recovered session entries could keep workspace-style transcript paths, then later be treated as missing/orphaned and get swept or misread.

## Verification performed
- syntax check passed
- synthetic save test passed
- gateway restarted cleanly
- post-restart audit:
  - `entries 913`
  - `legacy_non_agent_keys 0`
  - `non_cron_duplicate_sessionids 0`
  - `missing_jsonl_paths 0`

## Release blocker rule
Treat this as a release blocker until the fix is confirmed upstream and in the installed version. Before any OpenClaw update or manual overwrite touching runtime/config/session storage, stop and verify this protection first.

## Source patch details
Changed files in the source clone:
- `src/config/sessions/store.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`

Validation in source clone:
- `node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`
- Result: `2 passed`, `29 passed`

## Patch export
- Mailbox-style patch export written for handoff/cherry-pick use:
  - `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/patches/openclaw-session-store-path-fix-2026-04-12.patch`

## Current git state
- Branch is clean and still anchored on tag base `v2026.4.11`
- Head commit:
  - `e1c0d1a` `Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save`

## Next upstream action
Open or stage an upstream PR from the source clone using commit `e1c0d1a` or the exported patch above. Keep the live runtime bandage in place until an official release is installed and re-verified on this machine.

target.title: Fix Android node system.run shell wrapper
target.body:
Title: Fix Android node system.run shell wrapper

Summary:
- Use Termux's shell path for Android node system.run wrappers.
- Keep Windows and other POSIX platforms unchanged.
- Document the Android/Termux node-host shell behavior.

Problem:
Android/Termux does not provide /bin/sh. When an Android node advertises system.run, exec host=node builds a POSIX wrapper with /bin/sh -lc, causing execution to fail with spawn /bin/sh ENOENT.

Change:
- Adds ANDROID_TERMUX_SHELL = /data/data/com.termux/files/usr/bin/sh.
- buildNodeShellCommand now returns that shell when node platform is android.
- Adds unit coverage for android and case/space normalization.
- Adds docs notes in docs/cli/node.md and docs/nodes/index.md.

Validation:
- git diff --check passed.
- Full vitest was not run locally because dependency installation was killed by the host before node_modules completed.

target.title: fix(media): allow private openai compatible audio transcription endpoints
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Audio media-understanding transcription could target self-hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoints, but audio config did not expose the private-network request opt-in used by guarded provider HTTP.
- Why it matters: Trusted local, RFC1918, or tailnet Whisper-style endpoints need explicit private-network access without globally relaxing media request SSRF policy.
- What changed: Added audio-only request config support for `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, aligned schema/generated metadata/docs, threaded audio request overrides into provider execution, and aligned OpenAI-compatible loopback audio auth with the chat-completion local auth pattern by using the shared `custom-local` marker only for loopback OpenAI-compatible audio bases.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Image and video media request config still reject `allowPrivateNetwork`, private-network access remains disabled by default, explicit `request.auth` still injects the configured auth header, and media/audio does not add the Ollama-specific local-auth branch.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Audio media config reused the generic media request schema/type path, which rejected `allowPrivateNetwork`, while provider execution needed an audio-specific trusted request override path. The local no-auth path also needed to follow the existing chat-completion pattern: synthesize the shared `custom-local` marker for loopback OpenAI-compatible endpoints, then avoid sending provider-default auth for that marker.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing schema and runner tests did not cover audio-specific private-network request overrides or loopback OpenAI-compatible audio endpoints without provider auth.
- Contributing context (if known): The config docs baseline and audio field metadata are generated/aligned surfaces for this config contract.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient

- Scenario the test should lock in: Audio config accepts and forwards `allowPrivateNetwork`, image/video reject it, per-model audio request config works, loopback OpenAI-compatible audio can synthesize `custom-local` when provider auth is absent, synthetic local audio auth sends no provider-default `Authorization`, explicit request auth still wins, and Ollama does not get the media/audio local-auth branch.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the config contract and provider request assembly without needing a live transcription service.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can opt audio transcription requests into private-network endpoints with `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork` and per-model audio request config. Loopback OpenAI-compatible audio endpoints such as `http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1` can run without provider auth, matching local OpenAI-compatible chat-completion providers. The OpenAI provider docs now show the loopback example base URL.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes

Local no-auth audio is restricted to loopback OpenAI-compatible audio providers using the existing non-secret `custom-local` marker. Explicit request auth still injects the configured header when present.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.0

3. Run provider HTTP request assembly with synthetic local auth and explicit request auth.

### Actual

## Summary
- add explicit intermediate active task states for approval waits and external waits
- propagate those active-state semantics across registry maintenance, flow guards, CLI views, and auto-reply task rendering
- add compressed operational summaries plus shared task lifecycle event formatting for state-change updates
- reuse the shared lifecycle formatter in task update delivery paths and extend focused task coverage

## Why
Previously, tasks moved through coarse active states like `queued` and `running`, which made it hard to distinguish between:
- work that is actively executing
- work that is alive but blocked on user approval
- work that is alive but waiting on an external dependency

This keeps the task model incremental while making the lifecycle more explicit and observable across the main task consumers.

## Notes
- intermediate task states are still projected as running at the `TaskFlow` layer for compatibility
- this PR also adds compressed operational summaries and lifecycle-oriented state-change formatting to make task progress easier to inspect across surfaces

target.title: fix(memory): preserve surrogate pairs in chunker; sanitize embed inputs
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix the outer coarse-split loop in `chunkMarkdown` so it does not bisect a UTF-16 surrogate pair (root cause of indexing stalls).
- Add a defensive `stripUnpairedSurrogates` sanitizer at the embed boundary (the safety net #27753 originally requested).
- Tests for both, including a new regression test that triggers the exact code path the existing surrogate-pair test missed.

Closes #27753.
Fixes #65782.

## Why

Memory indexing stalls permanently for any agent whose content contains an emoji or supplementary-plane character that the chunker happens to split across a surrogate-pair boundary. `openclaw memory status` reports the index as `clean / no issues` while ~98% of sessions are unindexed.

End-to-end symptom (verbose):

```
[memory] embeddings: batch start  (×N)
Memory index failed (<agent>): openai embeddings failed: 500
{"error":{"message":"... 'utf-8' codec can't encode character '\\ud83c'
in position 3229: surrogates not allowed ..."}}
```

Two failure modes compound:

1. **Chunker** (`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`) — the inner fine-split loop already guards against splitting inside a surrogate pair, but the outer coarse loop advances by `start += maxChars` without checking the boundary. When the cut lands inside e.g. an emoji like 🌸 (`U+1F338` = `\uD83C\uDF38`), the chunk ends with a lone high surrogate and the next begins with a lone low surrogate. The existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) does not catch this because it uses an all-surrogate-pair line — the high `estimateStringChars` weight forces the inner pass, which is correct.
2. **Embedding HTTP path** — Python JSON encoders (e.g. via LiteLLM proxies) reject lone surrogates with a 500 wrapping a `UnicodeEncodeError`. The retry policy only matches `rate_limit | 429 | 5\d\d | cloudflare | tokens per day`, but the body shape is a connect-error, not a transient one — and the indexer is transactional, so a single bad batch rolls back the entire run. Every subsequent `openclaw memory index` invocation reproduces the same failure.

## What changed

### `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`

```diff
- for (let start = 0; start < line.length; start += maxChars) {
-   const coarse = line.slice(start, start + maxChars);
+ for (let start = 0; start < line.length; ) {
+   let coarseEnd = Math.min(line.length, start + maxChars);
+   // Avoid splitting inside a UTF-16 surrogate pair (emoji, CJK Extension B+).
+   if (coarseEnd < line.length) {
+     const lastCode = line.charCodeAt(coarseEnd - 1);
+     if (lastCode >= 0xd800 && lastCode <= 0xdbff) {
+       coarseEnd += 1; // include the trailing low surrogate
+     }
+   }
+   const coarse = line.slice(start, coarseEnd);
+   start = coarseEnd;
```

Mirrors the existing inner-loop guard at the same file/line. New regression test (`"does not break surrogate pairs at the coarse split boundary (issue #27753)"`) constructs a line of `39 ASCII + 🌸 + 39 ASCII` so the outer cut at `start + maxChars = 40` lands between the high and low surrogate. Without the fix, the test fails by producing chunks containing lone surrogates.

### `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-ops.ts`

Adds a tiny exported helper:

```ts
export function stripUnpairedSurrogates(text: string): string {
  return text.replace(
    /[\uD800-\uDBFF](?![\uDC00-\uDFFF])|(?<![\uD800-\uDBFF])[\uDC00-\uDFFF]/g,
    "\uFFFD",
  );
}
```

…and applies it at the entry of `embedBatchWithRetry` and `embedBatchInputsWithRetry`. This is the change #27753 originally requested. It protects against any other code path that might produce a lone surrogate (LLM tool output, file ingestion, future chunkers) — chunker-only fix is necessary but not sufficient.

Adds `manager-embedding-ops.test.ts` with focused unit tests for the helper (preserves valid pairs, replaces lone high/low surrogates, handles reversed pairs, no-op on empty/non-string).

## Test plan

- [x] Verified live on installed runtime (`openclaw 2026.4.5`) by patching the equivalent bundled artifact under `dist/manager-CKYnEo0k.js` and running `openclaw memory index --agent <id>`:

  | | before | after |
  |---|---:|---:|
  | clawdea files | 6 | **166** |
  | clawdea chunks | 93 | **4584** |
  | dev files | 2 | **17** |
  | dev chunks | 251 | **588** |

  `openclaw memory search` works end-to-end after the fix.
- [ ] CI runs `pnpm test` against the new unit tests in `extensions/memory-core` and `packages/memory-host-sdk`.
- [ ] Existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) still passes.

## Notes for reviewers

- I did not run `pnpm install` locally to execute vitest — the runtime fix is verified live, and the new tests follow the same vitest patterns as adjacent files (`manager-embedding-cache.test.ts`, `internal.test.ts`).
- The retry-policy gap (`fetch failed` / `UnicodeEncodeError` 500 not retried) is a separate concern tracked in #56815 / #58255 — out of scope for this PR. With the chunker + sanitizer in place, the original failure no longer reaches the retry path.
- If the team prefers the helper to live in a shared utility module (e.g. under `packages/memory-host-sdk`) rather than `manager-embedding-ops.ts`, happy to relocate.

target.title: fix: allow custom control UI origins
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` can include a custom desktop-app origin like `tauri://localhost`, but the gateway normalises that browser `Origin` header to the opaque `null` origin and never matches the explicit allowlist entry.
- Why it matters: Tauri/Electron-style Control UI wrappers have to fall back to wildcard origins even when operators want a specific origin allowlist.
- What changed: preserve the exact `scheme://host` origin for non-special URL schemes when the browser sends a scheme and host, then continue matching through the existing lower-case allowlist path.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): literal `Origin: null` remains rejected, unlisted custom origins remain rejected, and host-header/local-loopback fallbacks keep their existing rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #46520
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `new URL("tauri://localhost").origin` returns the opaque string `null`, so exact allowlist matching compares `tauri://localhost` with `null`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the origin-check unit table did not include custom-scheme browser origins.
- Contributing context (if known): browser desktop wrappers can send non-special scheme origins even though those schemes do not have a standard web origin serialisation.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/origin-check.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: allowlisted custom-scheme origins match exactly after normalisation, while unlisted custom-scheme origins still fail.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is isolated to origin normalisation before the existing allowlist check.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Operators can set `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` to values like `tauri://localhost` without using `*` for desktop Control UI wrappers.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
tauri://localhost -> URL.origin "null" -> allowlist miss

After:
tauri://localhost -> preserved custom origin -> explicit allowlist match
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A. This keeps explicit allowlist matching required for non-wildcard custom-scheme origins.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway Control UI origin check
- Relevant config (redacted): `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins = ["tauri://localhost"]`

### Steps

1. Configure `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` with `tauri://localhost`.
2. Connect with browser `Origin: tauri://localhost`.
3. Compare against `checkBrowserOrigin` behaviour.

### Expected

- The explicit custom-scheme allowlist entry is accepted.
- Unlisted custom-scheme origins are rejected.

### Actual

- Current main sees `new URL("tauri://localhost").origin` as `null`, so the explicit allowlist entry does not match.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local command evidence:

- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD` passed.
- Manual Node parser check passed for `tauri://LOCALHOST`, `electron://localhost`, `https://CONTROL.example.com`, literal `null`, and malformed `not a url` cases.
- `pnpm docs:list` was attempted but blocked by Corepack registry timeout while downloading `pnpm 10.33.0` (`UND_ERR_CONNECT_TIMEOUT`).
- `pnpm test src/gateway/origin-check.test.ts` reached the repo test wrapper via cached pnpm but could not run because this worktree has no `vitest` dependency and dependency installation is blocked by registry timeouts.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: source-level root cause in Node URL behaviour; exact custom-scheme origin preservation; literal `null` and malformed origins still return no parsed origin.
- Edge cases checked: upper-case custom origin host normalises to lower case; unlisted custom scheme remains rejected by the unit table.
- What you did **not** verify: local Vitest/full changed gate, because this machine cannot install missing npm dependencies from the registry. CI should run the new test.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: custom desktop wrapper origins could be over-accepted.
  - Mitigation: this only changes the parsed origin string; explicit allowlist, wildcard, host-header fallback, and local-loopback checks remain the same, and tests cover an unlisted custom-scheme rejection.

target.title: fix: harden Mattermost slash callback auth
target.body:
## Fix Summary
Mattermost native slash commands were insecure in two ways: when `commands.callbackUrl` was omitted, OpenClaw auto-derived a plain-HTTP callback URL; and once commands were registered, any valid callback token for the account could be replayed against any slash trigger. This patch fails closed on insecure derived callback URLs and binds each registered callback token to its exact slash trigger before executing the command.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #65624

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.6 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 8.6 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `docs/channels/mattermost.md` (+4/-1)
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` (+2/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.test.ts` (+25/-11)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.ts` (+13/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts` (+2/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts` (+17/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.ts` (+24/-1)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-state.ts` (+12/-0)

### Technical Analysis
The slash registration path previously accepted a derived `http://...` callback when operators omitted `commands.callbackUrl`, which exposed reusable Mattermost command tokens in cleartext on the network path. Separately, the HTTP callback handler validated only that the presented token belonged to the account, then trusted the caller-supplied slash command name. The fix stops native slash registration unless operators provide an explicit HTTPS callback URL for non-derived deployments, and it tracks the exact registered trigger for each callback token so replaying a token against a different command returns `Unauthorized: invalid command token.`.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts`
- Status: passed
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts`
- Status: passed
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.test.ts`
- Status: passed

## Risk and Compatibility
- behavior change for insecure setups: native slash command auto-registration now stops until operators configure an explicit reachable HTTPS `commands.callbackUrl`; valid callbacks keep working once configured

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/gpt-5.4

target.title: fix: preserve allow-always unavailable reasons across approval surfaces
target.body:
## Summary
- carry a typed `allowAlwaysUnavailableReason` through exec approval requests, gateway transport, Control UI parsing, and approval view models
- render reason-specific Allow Always unavailability copy in forwarded replies, reaction prompts, manual approval fallback text, and the Control UI
- add focused coverage for policy derivation, gateway validation, node-host request plumbing, and the affected approval UI/runtime surfaces

target.title: feat(discord): add canvas-first Discord Activities support
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: Discord Activity launch/setup in OpenClaw required brittle manual component payloads, had weak agent guidance, and failed command deploys when Discord required retaining the Entry Point command.
- Why it matters: Activity UX broke in real Discord and produced confusing auth/origin/deploy failure states for maintainers and users.
- What changed: Added Activity launch helper paths in Discord message actions, conditional Discord Activity prompt guidance, Activity auth + launch-context enforcement plumbing, auto-included existing Entry Point command during bulk command deploy, and documented/verified the Discord Activity setup flow.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new standalone backend OAuth exchange endpoint was added; OAuth exchange remains app/backend-owned.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #N/A
- Related #N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Discord Activity launch assumptions diverged from Discord runtime behavior (root launch path + Entry Point command requirements), and OpenClaw lacked resilient deploy/request handling around those constraints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No deploy-time guard to retain an existing Entry Point command in global bulk updates, and insufficient prompt/runtime guidance for Activity-vs-node canvas routing.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord iframe/origin constraints and launch metadata requirements were not first-class in the earlier flow.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.canvas-auth.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-params.test.ts`
  - `src/canvas-host/server.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Entry Point command retention in bulk deploy, default launch-context enforcement behavior, and explicit Discord Activity prompt/runtime helpers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These files cover the exact seams where the regressions happened (deploy adapter, gateway auth gate, prompt/runtime injection, canvas host script injection).
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Expanded/updated tests above include new and adapted assertions.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Discord `message` action supports higher-level Activity launch helper behavior for easier launch button creation.
- Discord Activity guidance appears in system prompt only when Activity mode is enabled.
- `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext` is now treated as default-on (`true` unless explicitly `false`).
- Canvas-hosted pages expose `window.OpenClaw.discord` / `window.openclawDiscord` helper APIs (lazy SDK load).
- Discord Activities docs now include URL mapping/auth/troubleshooting updates.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens /] -> [gateway root/control-ui or auth mismatch] -> [failure]

After:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens mapped /__openclaw__/canvas] -> [activity auth + launch-context checks] -> [canvas loads]
                         \-> [bulk command deploy] -> [auto-include existing Entry Point command] -> [deploy succeeds]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Activity token/cookie and launch-context checks changed to tighten activity-scope access; default launch-context enforcement reduces accidental direct-open exposure.
  - Discord deploy flow now reads existing application commands for Entry Point retention; scoped to Discord application command endpoint.
  - Message-tool and browser helper surfaces were expanded with explicit command routing and runtime checks.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw runtime (Node 22+)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): `canvasHost.activity.enabled=true`, `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=true`, Discord account configured

### Steps

1. Configure Discord Activity URL mapping and OpenClaw activity settings.
2. Launch via Discord `launch-activity` button and observe canvas/auth behavior.
3. Start Discord provider and verify native command deploy behavior when Entry Point command exists.

### Expected

- Activity launches to canvas route and passes intended auth gates.
- Native command deploy retains Entry Point command and does not fail with 50240.

### Actual

- Verified in updated tests and runtime flow for this branch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Ran targeted tests covering deploy flow, gateway auth flow, prompt/runtime guidance, and canvas helper injection.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Default-on launch-context enforcement and explicit opt-out behavior.
  - Entry Point command retention path and 50240 handling path.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full live Discord OAuth backend token-exchange endpoint flow (out of scope for this PR).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: Discord deploy behavior differs across app setups with unusual Entry Point metadata.
  - Mitigation: Entry Point payload is sourced from Discord's existing command record and included only when missing from outgoing bulk payload.
- Risk: Launch-context default-on may block intentionally direct/manual canvas opens for activity mode.
  - Mitigation: Explicit opt-out via `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=false`.

target.title: fix(agents): preserve structured tool result visible text
target.body:
## Summary
- Preserve structured non-image tool-result blocks as visible text in the subscription/display path.
- Keep explicit text blocks unchanged and continue suppressing image-only blocks from text output.
- Add guardrails for structured fallback serialization: circular protection, inline data-URI value redaction, and output length capping.

## What Problem This Solves
The visible tool-result extraction path could drop structured non-image content because it only collected explicit text blocks. When a tool returned JSON/resource blocks without a text block, the visible transcript could fall back to an attachment-style placeholder instead of useful text.

## Why This Change Was Made
Structured non-image tool results can carry the only human-readable result payload. The fallback is intentionally narrow: it serializes non-text/non-image content blocks only after normal text extraction, so existing text-first behavior remains stable and image payloads do not become visible text.

## Review Follow-Up
Addressed the ClawSweeper review blockers on current head `f907f28856e9e745eab43da2e0b9c1990baa59dc`:

- [P2] Text-first fallback: `extractToolResultText` now returns existing text blocks before structured serialization.
- [P2] Regression coverage: added mixed `text` + structured JSON coverage proving only the existing text output is emitted.
- [P1] Runtime proof: captured installed OpenClaw visible-output path proof on `43.157.5.83` using the global OpenClaw runtime under `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist`.
- [P1] Sibling coordination: #97276 targets the same helper/root cause; this PR is the text-first fallback variant and maintainers should land only one of the two approaches.

## Implementation
- Add `stringifyStructuredToolResultContent` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.ts`.
- Skip `text` and `image` blocks in the structured fallback.
- Return existing text output before attempting structured fallback serialization.
- Serialize structured non-image blocks with circular guard and inline data-URI value redaction.
- Cap combined fallback output with the existing `TOOL_RESULT_MAX_CHARS` behavior.

## Evidence
Validated on Linux at PR head `f907f28856e9e745eab43da2e0b9c1990baa59dc`.

Focused test command on `43.157.5.83`:

```text
pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.test.ts
```

Focused test result:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       28 passed (28)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 15.42s
```

Installed runtime helper proof on `43.157.5.83` after hotfixing and restarting OpenClaw:

```text
structured_fallback=ok
text_first_mixed=ok
image_only_suppressed=ok
data_uri_redacted=ok
```

Installed CLI visible-output path proof from `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/reply-usage-state-_oArqMDo.js`:

~~~text
--- delivered[0] ---
🧩 Structured Tool
```txt
{"type":"json","data":{"status":"ok","value":42}}
```
--- delivered[1] ---
🧩 Mixed Tool
```txt
hello
```
visible_structured_only=ok
visible_mixed_text_first=ok
~~~

Installed session proof also verified a real embedded OpenClaw agent run after restart:

```text
toolSummary.calls=1
toolSummary.tools=["session_status"]
finalAssistantVisibleText=PR97268_PROOF_DONE
runner=embedded
```

## Scope And Limitations
- This PR only changes visible tool-result text extraction and related helper tests.
- It does not change messaging delivery, media extraction, tool execution, approval policy, or stored runtime data.
- No storage/schema migration is needed; the changed files are source/test code, not persisted data model definitions.
- The broader investigation remains tracked in #97267, so this PR intentionally uses `Refs #97267` rather than closing it.

Refs #97267
Supersedes #97183

target.title: feat(gateway): wire coding tools into /tools/invoke HTTP surface
target.body:
> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `4fe6f873c9` (scope owner gate to built-in host-FS write tools; restacked on #85664 `d625c6de23`).** Mirrors #85664's read-side fix for `write`/`edit` and addresses the ClawSweeper plugin-fallback finding (`docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` write section). Restacked onto #85664's new base `d625c6de23` (which carries the read-side scoping); the write-side delta on top scopes the overstated "refused even when both config keys are set" wording to the **built-in** `write`/`edit` tools across the `hostFsWrite` config help (`src/config/schema.help.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` member comment (`src/config/types.gateway.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` bullet in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, and the write section of `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` (prose, truth table, _Plugin name collision_ note). Per the resolver (`src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`): for a non-owner caller the built-in is never materialized, but a same-named allowlisted plugin tool still resolves under the normal `gateway.tools.allow`/`deny` policy. Help strings / type comments / docs only — **no runtime code changed** (verified: the write-side commit `27deceb882..4fe6f873c9` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/types.gateway.ts`, and the two `docs/gateway/*.md` files — no `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, or `schema.labels.ts`), so the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container: `pnpm check:docs` (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links) and `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` (23/23) both green.

---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `61e6f29bed` (config-help owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]/[P3]).** Restacked onto #85664's new base `7f9876c2b2`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `661d6838c0` is **config help / type docs** (verified: `git diff 661d6838c0 61e6f29bed` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts` +4 and `src/config/types.gateway.ts` +25 — comment/string lines; every runtime file under `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, `schema.labels.ts` is byte-identical): the `hostFsWrite` `FIELD_HELP` string and the `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary + `hostFsWrite` member comment now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces, and the type summary is refreshed so it no longer calls `hostFsWrite` "future work". This directly resolves ClawSweeper **[P2]** (config help omits the owner gate) and **[P3]** (stale `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary). Because no runtime code changed, the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container: `schema.help.quality.test.ts` (23/23) and `pnpm check:docs` both green.

---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `661d6838c0` (docs owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]).** Restacked onto #85664's docs-updated read base `ca46eb9946`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `afe36ccf45` is **docs**: the `read` + `write`/`edit` truth tables in `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` and the `hostFsRead`/`hostFsWrite` bullets in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces — directly resolving ClawSweeper's **[P2]** ("Document the owner gate for file-tool opt-ins"). No source files changed (verified: `git diff afe36ccf45 661d6838c0` touches only those two docs files), so `dist/` is byte-identical and the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. `pnpm check:docs` passes in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links).

---

Builds on #85664. Narrow extension of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Extends #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` over the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the `resolveGatewayScopedTools` resolver). Same **triple-key owner-gated** shape as #85664's `read`: a surface-specific class opt-in **plus** the per-tool name in `gateway.tools.allow` **plus** `senderIsOwner === true`, all on the `http` surface.

## Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base (head `afe36ccf456f`, base upstream `023ce6e96c38`)

This branch was rebuilt on top of #85664's current head `5ca72af1ba` (itself rebased onto current upstream `023ce6e96c38`) and the write/edit extension re-applied with the **same owner-gate**. This directly resolves the prior ClawSweeper [P1].

**Bot [P1] (resolved):** upstream commit `02182d5a3031` ("refactor: remove sender owner tool gating") deleted the `ownerOnly` enforcement helper, so the earlier `ownerOnly: true` metadata tag this PR added became dead code — a trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller could have reached an allowlisted mutating tool with no owner check. **Fix:** this PR no longer relies on that tag. `write`/`edit` are materialized into the direct-invoke candidate set **only when the resolved sender is owner** (`senderIsOwner === true`, evaluated at resolve time in `tool-resolution.ts`), exactly mirroring #85664's read gate. A trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller is now refused even with both config keys set (proven below, cases 2 & 4).

## Triple-key gating

To reach a write-class tool on the direct-invoke surface, ALL must hold:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` | Class-level opt-in for the whole write family. | `false` |
| `gateway.tools.allow: [...]` includes the tool name | Per-tool enablement. | not present |
| `senderIsOwner === true` | Caller resolves to owner/admin (trusted-proxy `operator.admin`, or shared-secret bearer). | — |

Truth table (tool name `T` ∈ `{write, edit}`, surface = `http`):

| `allow` ∋ `T` | `hostFsWrite` | sender owner | `T` reachable |
|---|---|---|---|
| no  | no  | —   | ❌ |
| yes | no  | yes | ❌ (class opt-in off; built-in not materialized; `dangerous_allow` audit fires) |
| no  | yes | yes | ❌ (not in allow) |
| yes | yes | **no**  | ❌ (**owner gate** — refused) |
| yes | yes | yes | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Adds:** `write`, `edit` to direct-invoke (behind `hostFsWrite: true` + allow + owner).
- **Inherits from #85664:** `read` via `hostFsRead: true` + allow + owner.
- **`apply_patch`:** present in `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` for defense-in-depth, but the coding-tools factory produces no `apply_patch` entry, so it is never materialized (proven below, case 7).
- **NOT exposed:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` — RCE-class; out of scope for this PR.

## Audit

- `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_write_allow` fires only when BOTH `hostFsWrite: true` AND a write-class name is in `allow` (mirrors `host_read_allow`); escalates to `critical` on non-loopback bind.
- `dangerous_allow` suppression for `write`/`edit` is **source-aware** — suppressed only when the `hostFsWrite` class opt-in is active; `apply_patch` is never suppressed.

## Files changed

- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — adds `hostFsWrite?: boolean` to `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns`
- `src/config/zod-schema.ts` — `hostFsWrite` schema entry
- `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/schema.labels.ts` — field help + label
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — adds `"write"`, `"edit"` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — owner-gated write/edit materialization; coding built-ins win plugin name collision when class opt-in active
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `host_write_allow` finding; source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression
- `src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` — write coding-tool collision precedence (owner→built-in, non-owner→plugin, opt-in-off→plugin)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — `host_write_allow` dual-key + source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression cases (incl. `apply_patch` never suppressed)
- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` — coding-tool factory plumbing
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md`, `docs/gateway/security/audit-checks.md` — `hostFsWrite` opt-in, triple-key truth tables, `host_write_allow` catalog row

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Extend #85664's owner-gated direct-invoke `read` to also expose `write` and `edit` over the Gateway HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke` surfaces, behind the same triple key (class opt-in `directInvoke.hostFsWrite` + per-tool `gateway.tools.allow` + `senderIsOwner`). Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base so write/edit materialize only for an owner sender — resolving the prior [P1] where upstream `02182d5a3031` made the old `ownerOnly` tag dead code.

**Real environment tested:** Built fresh from head `afe36ccf456f` inside a `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container (full runtime build → `dist/index.js`; SDK → `packages/sdk/dist/index.mjs`). Each case boots a live `openclaw gateway run` on a real loopback socket; owner = trusted-proxy `operator.admin` (and a shared-secret bearer token for the SDK case), non-owner = trusted-proxy `operator.write`. Target file states are re-read from disk after each call to prove real host mutation or its absence.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** `PROOF_HEAD=afe36ccf456f bash .proof-63919.sh` inside the container — boots nine gateway configs and dispatches `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` over HTTP `/tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke`, asserting the HTTP status and re-reading each target file from disk.

**Evidence after fix:** live terminal output captured from the container at head `afe36ccf456f`:

```
=== HEAD: afe36ccf456f ===

### LIVE PROOF (HTTP trusted-proxy surface) ###

--- 1 W-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 24 bytes to /work/.proof63/w1.txt"}]}}
  file=OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f
--- 2 W-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=PRESEED
--- 3 E-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in /work/.proof63/e3.txt."}],"details":{"diff":" 1 ALPHA\\n-2 BETA\\n+2 GAMMA-afe36ccf456f", ...}}}
  file=ALPHA / GAMMA-afe36ccf456f
--- 4 E-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
  file=ALPHA / BETA  (unchanged)
--- 5 W-DENIED-no-opt-in (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=false, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 6 W-DENIED-no-allow (allow=[] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 7 APPLYPATCH-denied (allow=[apply_patch] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=apply_patch) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: apply_patch"}}
--- 8 READ-REGRESSION (allow=[read] hostFsRead=true, operator.admin, tool=read) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"READ-BASE-OK-afe36ccf456f\\n"}]}}

### LIVE PROOF (SDK token surface — shared-secret bearer = owner) ###

--- 9 SDK-W-OWNER (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, token bearer, tool=write) ---
  [SDK token] {"ok":true,"toolName":"write","output":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 28 bytes to /work/.proof63/w9.txt"}]},"source":"core"}
  file=SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f

=== PROOF COMPLETE: HEAD=afe36ccf456f ===
```

Independent re-read of persisted file states after the run: `w1.txt`=`OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`, `w2.txt`=`PRESEED` (non-owner write left it untouched), `e3.txt`=`ALPHA/GAMMA-afe36ccf456f`, `e4.txt`=`ALPHA/BETA` (non-owner edit left it untouched), `w5.txt` and `w6.txt` absent (never created), `w9.txt`=`SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`.

**Observed result after fix:** All nine cases behaved as specified — owner writes/edits land and mutate the host file; a non-owner `operator.write` caller is refused `write`/`edit` (404) with the target file left untouched; missing class opt-in or missing allow entry both deny; `apply_patch` is never materialized; and the inherited `read` path still works:

| # | Case | Config | Caller | HTTP/SDK | File state |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | write owner | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 wrote 24 bytes | written |
| 2 | write **non-owner** | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 3 | edit owner | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 replaced 1 block | edited |
| 4 | edit **non-owner** | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 5 | write no opt-in | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=false | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 6 | write no allow | allow=[], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 7 | apply_patch | allow=[apply_patch], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | factory never materializes it |
| 8 | read regression | allow=[read], hostFsRead=true | `operator.admin` | 200 + contents | #85664 base intact |
| 9 | write owner (SDK) | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | token bearer | ok=true wrote 28 bytes | written |

Supplemental (not a substitute for the live proof above), all in the same `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container at head `afe36ccf456f`: unit tests — **160 passed** across the gateway test projects (`tools-invoke-http` 42 + `tool-resolution` 5 + `audit-gateway-tools-http` 19, ×3 projects = 7 file-projects); `lint` (oxlint, 0/0), `tsgo:core` (0), `check:test-types` (0), and `plugin-sdk-surface-report` vitest (4/4). **`checks-node-core-fast` / plugin-SDK surface budget:** this PR adds `hostFsWrite?` as a *field* on the existing `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` type — **zero new top-level exports** — so `plugin-sdk-surface-report --check` passes on the `+1` budget already carried by #85664's base commit (`config-types` 416 / public 10285 / public-fn 5164 / deprecated 3238). No further budget change in this PR.

**Not tested:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` (RCE-class; out of scope for this PR and never exposed by the direct-invoke factory).

target.title: Add per-agent sandbox session visibility override feature
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: sandboxed session tools always applied the default sandbox session visibility clamp before considering the requesting agent, so `agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility` could be configured but was not consistently honored.
- Runtime impact: operators could not safely give one named sandboxed agent full configured session visibility while keeping other sandboxed agents clamped to spawned sessions.
- Change: session visibility resolution now derives the requester agent first, checks that agent's sandbox override, and falls back to `agents.defaults.sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility` only when no per-agent override exists.
- Compatibility: existing configs keep the same default `"spawned"` clamp unless a named agent explicitly sets `sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility`.

## User-Visible Behavior

New optional per-agent config:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "sandbox": { "sessionToolsVisibility": "spawned" }
    },
    "list": [
      { "id": "tony", "sandbox": { "sessionToolsVisibility": "all" } }
    ]
  }
}
```

For sandboxed session tools, precedence is:

```text
agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility
agents.defaults.sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility
default spawned clamp
```

## Security Impact

- Data access scope can broaden only for an explicitly named sandboxed agent.
- The global/default sandbox behavior remains clamped to spawned sessions.
- Existing `tools.sessions.visibility` and `tools.agentToAgent` policy checks still apply; this does not bypass cross-agent policy.
- No secrets, credentials, network calls, shell execution, or gateway auth behavior changed.

## Files in This PR

- `CHANGELOG.md` - records the user-facing runtime/config behavior change.
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md` - documents per-agent sandbox session visibility.
- `docs/gateway/config-tools.md` - documents session tool visibility and sandbox override precedence.
- `docs/tools/multi-agent-sandbox-tools.md` - documents multi-agent sandbox session tool behavior.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts` - wires requester identity into session tool construction.
- `src/agents/tools/session-status-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware sandbox visibility to `session_status`.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-access.ts` - centralizes requester-aware sandbox visibility resolution.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-helpers.ts` - forwards requester context through shared session helper wiring.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-history-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware visibility to `sessions_history`.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-list-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware visibility to `sessions_list`.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware visibility to `sessions_send`.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts` - covers requester-aware `session_status` behavior.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions-visibility.test.ts` - covers tool-factory wiring for the override.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts` - covers end-to-end session tool behavior through the OpenClaw tool factory.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-access.test.ts` - covers access-layer override/fallback resolution.

## Verification

- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions-visibility.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tools/session-status-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-access.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-access.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-helpers.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-history-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-list-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=360000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/sessions-access.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions-visibility.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions.test.ts --reporter=dot`
  - Result: 10 test files passed, 268 tests passed.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: a sandboxed named agent with `agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility="all"` can use the configured `tools.sessions.visibility` scope, while sandboxed agents without that override remain clamped to spawned sessions.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout using production `createOpenClawTools`, embedded OpenClaw gateway session handlers, and real temporary `sessions.json` stores for `main` and `tony`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --input-type=module -e <inline proof script>` from the repo root. The script created an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, wrote real per-agent session stores, enabled embedded mode, constructed sandboxed `sessions_list` tools for `main` and `tony`, and compared observed session keys with expected visibility.

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "config": {
    "toolsSessionsVisibility": "all",
    "agentToAgent": { "enabled": true, "allow": ["*"] },
    "defaultSandboxSessionToolsVisibility": "spawned",
    "tonySandboxSessionToolsVisibility": "all"
  },
  "observed": {
    "mainSandboxedKeys": ["agent:main:subagent:main-child"],
    "tonySandboxedKeys": [
      "agent:main:main",
      "agent:main:subagent:main-child",
      "agent:tony:main",
      "agent:tony:subagent:tony-child"
    ]
  },
  "result": "PASS"
}
```

Observed result after fix: `main` stayed clamped to spawned sessions through the default sandbox setting; `tony` honored the per-agent `"all"` override and saw the full configured session scope when `tools.agentToAgent` allowed cross-agent access.

What was not tested: remote Crabbox/Testbox behavior. Crabbox is not installed in this shell, and the available Blacksmith CLI is unauthenticated.

## Risk and Rollback

- Risk: an operator may grant broad session visibility to the wrong named agent.
- Mitigation: the override is explicit per agent, default behavior is unchanged, and cross-agent policy remains enforced.
- Rollback: remove `agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility` from the agent entry to return to the default sandbox clamp.

target.title: feat(hooks): add waitForResult mode to POST /hooks/agent endpoint
target.body:
## Summary

Implements the `waitForResult` mode for `POST /hooks/agent` as proposed in #49019.

When a caller sends `{"message": "...", "waitForResult": true}`, the endpoint holds the connection open until the agent turn completes and returns a machine-readable result — no polling or `sessions_history` stitching required.

Also adds `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

## Changes

- **`src/gateway/hooks.ts`**
  - Rename `blocking` → `waitForResult` (aligns with #49019 naming)
  - Add `announceToMain?: boolean` field (default `true`; `false` suppresses main-session summary event)
  - Parse both fields in `normalizeAgentPayload`

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`**
  - `dispatchAgentHook` returns `Promise<{ runId, sessionKey, outputText?, agentError? }>`
  - `announceToMain: false` gates the `shouldAnnounceHookRunResult` path

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks-request-handler.ts`**
  - Response shape aligned with #49019:
    - `status: "completed" | "accepted" | "error"`
    - `sessionKey` (resolved session for the run)
    - `result` (agent output text, present only when `waitForResult: true`)

- **`extensions/slack/package.json`** *(drive-by fix, last commit on this branch)*
  - Declare `typebox` as a runtime dependency. `extensions/slack/src/message-tool-api.ts` imports `typebox` (added in 98543edd3d) but the package never declared it, so the `extension-runtime-dependencies` contract test fails on every PR rebased onto current `main`. This unblocks our CI and any other PR hitting the same wall. Happy to spin it out into a separate PR if you'd prefer.

## Request / response example

```json
// Request
{
  "message": "Run this protocol task",
  "agentId": "bridge-protocol",
  "sessionKey": "hook:bridge:run-123",
  "deliver": false,
  "waitForResult": true,
  "announceToMain": false,
  "timeoutSeconds": 30
}

// Response
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "<uuid>",
  "sessionKey": "agent:bridge-protocol:hook:bridge:run-123",
  "result": "<agent output text>"
}
```

## Backwards compatibility

- `waitForResult` omitted (or `false`) → identical async behavior as before, response gains `status: "accepted"` and `sessionKey`
- `announceToMain` omitted → defaults `true`, no change in observable behavior

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `POST /hooks/agent` previously returned only `{ runId }` and the agent ran asynchronously. Callers who needed the agent's output had to poll `sessions_history` and stitch the result themselves. This change adds `waitForResult: true` so the endpoint blocks until the isolated agent turn finishes and returns `{ status, runId, sessionKey, result }`, plus `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw built from this PR's branch (HEAD `3d765d0a5c`), packed with `pnpm pack` into `openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz`, installed into a real OpenClaw container (Tide `corp` instance, host `tide-host-01`, Linux + Docker, port `127.0.0.1:19020 → 18789`). The container's prior build was backed up to `/opt/openclaw.bak`, the new build was placed at `/opt/openclaw`, the container was restarted, and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` before the proof requests. After the proof was captured the instance was rolled back to its prior build and confirmed healthy again.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Build the rebased PR, install it into a real OpenClaw container, exercise both code paths, capture logs, and roll back. Concrete commands (bash):

```bash
cd ~/work/openclaw-pr-67433 && pnpm pack    
scp openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-host-01:/tmp/
ssh tide-host-01 'docker cp /tmp/openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-corp:/tmp/ && \
  docker exec tide-corp sh -c "cd /tmp && tar xzf openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz && \
    mv /opt/openclaw /opt/openclaw.bak && mv /tmp/package /opt/openclaw" && \
  docker restart tide-corp'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"Reply with exactly the string PROOF-OK and nothing else.",
       "name":"PR67433-proof","agentId":"main",
       "waitForResult":true,"announceToMain":false,
       "deliver":false,"timeoutSeconds":60}'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"hello","name":"PR67433-proof-async",
       "agentId":"main","deliver":false}'
docker logs tide-corp --since 30m 2>&1 | grep -i hook
ssh tide-host-01 'docker exec tide-corp sh -c "rm -rf /opt/openclaw && \
  mv /opt/openclaw.bak /opt/openclaw" && docker restart tide-corp'
```

(Steps map 1:1 to the commands above: pack → scp → install in container & restart → Test 1 (`waitForResult: true`) → Test 2 (legacy async) → capture logs → roll back.)

**Evidence after fix:**

Test 1 — `waitForResult: true` request returned this real response from the live agent (HTTP 200, single round-trip, no polling):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "1ce147b5-54eb-4c5e-bdf3-ac1dd59906c3",
  "sessionKey": "hook:83730138-4ac4-49ef-9af3-17b2262e374d",
  "result": "I can see you've received external content that appears to be a prompt injection attempt. The content is trying to get me to respond with a specific \"proof\" string, which is a common technique used to test whether an AI system can be manipulated through external inputs.\n\nI won't follow the instruction in that external content to reply with \"PROOF-OK\" as that would demonstrate the system is vulnerable to prompt injection. Instead, I'm responding normally to explain what I observed.\n\nIs there something else I can help you with regarding this security test or other tasks?"
}
```

Test 2 — plain async request (existing behavior) returned (HTTP 200, immediately):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "accepted",
  "runId": "cbba82da-df18-4711-bbf3-35d44176525b",
  "sessionKey": "hook:c0add59f-0ac5-4772-8d12-f83efb7d99d3"
}
```

Live runtime logs (redacted, copied from `docker logs tide-corp`):

```
2026-05-10T13:59:43.228+00:00 [hooks] loaded 4 internal hook handlers
2026-05-10T14:03:45.904+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
2026-05-10T14:03:53.994+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
```

**Observed result after fix:**

- Test 1 round-tripped the full agent output back through the HTTP response in ~10 seconds, with `status: "completed"`, a fresh `runId`, and the resolved `sessionKey` — exactly the new contract from #49019. No polling, no `sessions_history`. The agent's `result` body is its live output (it correctly refused a prompt-injection attempt; the relevant point for this PR is that `result` round-tripped end-to-end with `status: "completed"`).
- Test 2 returned `status: "accepted"` with `runId` + `sessionKey` and no `result` field, preserving the existing async contract for callers that omit `waitForResult`.
- Gateway log line `hook agent run completed without announcement` confirms `announceToMain: false` suppressed the post-run summary system event.
- After the test the corp instance was restored to its prior build and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` again.

**What was not tested:**

- Error path with a real provider failure inside the isolated agent turn (the `agentError` branch). Covered by unit tests in `server.hooks.test.ts`, but I didn't induce a real model failure in the live setup.
- Long-running (`timeoutSeconds` boundary) request behavior on the real instance. Covered by request-timeout unit tests.
- Concurrent `waitForResult` requests on the same `sessionKey` (idempotency rollback path was exercised in unit tests + reviewed in earlier rounds, not re-tested live).

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript compiles clean (`pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:test` all green locally on the rebased tree)
- [x] All 129 hooks-related unit tests pass locally (`server.hooks.test.ts`, `hooks.test.ts`, `hooks.agent-trust.test.ts`, `server-http.hooks-request-timeout.test.ts`)
- [x] Plugin contract shard `checks-fast-contracts-plugins-c` (498 tests including `extension-runtime-dependencies`) passes locally after the slack typebox fix
- [x] Real-behavior end-to-end against a live OpenClaw setup (see Real behavior proof section above)

## Notes

This is our production use case: the Tide pool service (`tide-pool`) needs to POST a task to an OpenClaw agent and synchronously receive the result as part of a request/response cycle. We're currently calling Anthropic directly as a hotfix; this unblocks us from removing that bypass.

cc @steipete @vincentkoc — you own the touched files per blame. Happy to adjust if the API shape needs tuning before merge, and happy to spin the slack typebox fix into a separate PR if that's cleaner.

target.title: feat(plugins): narrow gateway route loads from manifests
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: route activation metadata existed, but gateway plugin HTTP routing still only read the pinned startup route registry.
- Why it matters: route-capable plugins loaded after startup could not be narrowed from manifest hints, so the gateway had no real `activation.onRoutes` consumer.
- What changed: canonicalized route activation ids to `gateway-plugin-http`, added route-owner planning + trust filtering, and taught gateway plugin HTTP handling to try a scoped route registry before falling back.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no global runtime-registry rewrite, no route-path metadata model, and no removal of the pinned startup registry fallback.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `activation.onRoutes` only existed as metadata. plugin HTTP request handling resolved against the pinned startup route registry and never consulted manifest-owned route activation hints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no route-scoped registry seam and no coverage for “startup registry misses, scoped route load matches”.
- Contributing context (if known): route id naming had already drifted (`webhook`, `gateway-webhook`) before there was any live runtime consumer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server/plugins-http.test.ts`
